this report investigates the critical application of data mining techniques
as the volume of digital information grows
exponentially, the ability to filter and personalize content becomes essential for user
this study explores four primary data mining
methodologies: association rule mining, clustering, classification, and sequential
furthermore, it analyzes how these techniques are integrated into rs
models and evaluated using standard metrics such as rmse, precision, and recall.
the findings demonstrate that data mining is the core computational engine driving
data mining methods for recommender systems
in the modern digital era, we are witnessing an unprecedented explosion of data.
from social media interactions to e-commerce transaction logs, the volume of
this phenomenon leads to a problem known
as "information overload," where users are overwhelmed by choices.
recommender systems (rs) have emerged as essential tools to filter noise and
data mining is defined as the computational process of discovering hidden,
useful, and actionable information within these large-scale datasets.
the extraction of data, but a method of utilizing sophisticated techniques and
algorithms from the field of data science to identify patterns, rules, or valuable
in information technology, data mining serves as the crucial bridge
between raw, unstructured data and actionable business knowledge.
1.2 the role of business intelligence
data mining is a fundamental subset of the broader business intelligence (bi)
real-world applications are ubiquitous across various industries:
• e-commerce: major platforms like shopee or amazon utilize mining
algorithms to analyze purchase history.
by understanding what a user has
bought, the system can recommend relevant products, thereby increasing
• streaming services: platforms such as netflix and youtube analyze
they track what you watch, how long
you watch, and what you skip to suggest new videos, keeping users
engaged on the platform for longer periods.
data mining methods for recommender systems
• finance: banks employ anomaly detection (a specialized form of data
mining) to identify irregular patterns indicative of fraudulent
transactions, ensuring security for users.
the integration of data mining into recommender systems is driven by two
1) data volume : the sheer volume of user data (logs, clicks, ratings)
2) accuracy requirements : withot sophisticated data mining, recommen
would be random or generic, leading to poor use satisfaction.
1.3 the general data mining process
to implement a robust recommender system, a structured five-step process is
figure 1: diagram of the 5-step data mining process
• data collection: this involves gathering raw data from various sources.
it includes user data (demographics), item data (attributes, metadata),
and interaction data (ratings, clicks, purchase history) .
data mining methods for recommender systems
• data preprocessing: raw data is often incomplete or noisy.
involves handling missing values (imputation) and removing noise to
• data mining: this is the core phase where algorithms such as clustering
or association rules are applied to discover patterns.
• model building: the discovered patterns are transformed into
predictive models, such as collaborative filtering or content-based
• evaluation: the system is tested using metrics to ensure reliability

chapter 2: advanced data mining techniques
2.1 association rule mining (market basket analysis)
association rule mining is a rule-based machine learning method for
discovering interesting relations between variables in large databases .
known as "market basket analysis."
figure 2: example of market basket analysis visualization
the fundamental rule is expressed in the form x → y.
• meaning: "if a customer buys item set x, there is a high probability they
• antecedent (x): the "if" part – the set of items present in the
• consequent (y): the "then" part – the set of items likely to appear with
to evaluate the strength of a rule, three key metrics are used:
data mining methods for recommender systems
support : measures how frequently the item set appears in the database.
confidence: measures the reliability of the inference.
conditional probability that a transaction containing x also contains y.
figure 4: formula for confidence
lift: measures the strength of the rule over random chance.
indicates a strong positive association.
• apriori : this algorithm generates frequent itemsets by creating
candidate sets and pruning those that do not meet the minimum support
it is simple but computationally expensive for large datasets.
• fp-growth (frequent pattern growth) : this method avoids
candidate generation by building an fp-tree structure.
improves performance compared to apriori, especially on large datasets.
• eclat (equivalence class clustering and bottom-up lattice
traversal) : this algorithm uses a vertical data format with transaction
id (tid) sets and discovers frequent itemsets through set intersection
data mining methods for recommender systems
clustering is an unsupervised learning technique that groups a set of objects in
such a way that objects in the same group (cluster) are more similar to each other than
to those in other groups.key tools in adobe indesign:
figure 6: clustering algorithm flowchart
• k-means: this algorithm partitions data into k distinct clusters.
by iterating to minimize the variance within each cluster until the
it is highly efficient for large datasets but
requires specifying k in advance .
• hierarchical clustering: this method builds a hierarchy of clusters,
either agglomerative (bottom-up) or divisive (top-down).
dendrogram which is useful for taxonomy visualization.
• dbscan (density-based spatial clustering of applications with
noise): unlike k-means, dbscan groups points based on density.
particularly powerful because it can detect outliers (noise) and find
data mining methods for recommender systems
classification is a supervised learning technique used to predict categorical
class labels for new data based on past observations.
is often used to predict whether a user will "like" or "dislike" an item.
figure 7: classfication algorithm flowchart
• decision trees: a flowchart-like structure where internal nodes
represent feature tests, and leaf nodes represent class labels.
is easy to interpret and visualize.
• knn (k-nearest neighbors): a "lazy learning" algorithm that
classifies a data point based on the majority class of its k nearest
neighbors in the feature space.
• logistic regression: a statistical model used for binary classification
(e.g., spam/not spam), estimating the probability of an event occurring.
2.4 sequential pattern mining (spm)
sequential pattern mining differs from standard association rules by
incorporating the dimension of time.
it discovers subsequences that appear frequently
data mining methods for recommender systems
consider the following transaction history:
• customer a: buy a → buy b, c → buy d
• customer b: buy a → buy c → buy d
• customer c: buy a → buy b → buy d
the algorithm identifies the common pattern: a → b → d. this implies that
customers who buy a often eventually buy d, even if not immediately.
• user behavior analysis : analyzing clickstreams (page a → page b
• healthcare : predicting symptom progression: symptom → diagnosis
• fraud detection : identifying sequences of actions that typically precede

3.1 mapping techniques to recommender models
different data mining techniques serve different types of recommender
the following mapping illustrates how these methods are applied in
sequential pattern mining → sequence-aware rs:
• description : predicts the next item based on time-ordered history.
• use case : next-song recommendation in music apps.
• description : recommends items with similar attributes to those a
• use case : suggesting news articles based on keywords.
clustering → user-based collaborative filtering:
• description : groups similar users to recommend items liked by the
• use case : "users who are similar to you also liked..."
association rule mining → knowledge-based rs:
• description : suggests items based on explicit rules (e.g., "people
to ensure the effectiveness of the recommender system, appropriate
evaluation metrics must be selected based on the algorithm used.
• for association rules: metrics include support , confidence , and
data mining methods for recommender systems
• for clustering: the silhouette coefficient is used to measure
cluster quality, along with precision@k for recommendation
• for classification: standard metrics include precision (exactness),
recall (completeness), and auc (area under the curve) .
• for sequential mining: metrics focus on rank accuracy, such as
recall@k and mrr (mean reciprocal rank).
evaluations are typically conducted in two phases:
• offline evaluation: using historical datasets to calculate rmse or
• online evaluation: real-time testing using a/b testing, click-through
rate (ctr), and conversion rate.
